- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- spec-human: Create a human-reviewable architecture spec using a 6-section template (Summary, Background, Architecture, Interface, Edge Cases, Alternatives) BEFORE implementing a non-trivial design. Triggered explicitly via /spec-human <topic>, or when the user says "write a human spec", "create a review-ready design doc", "spec this out for review", or "I need a spec for X before we build it". Produces an ASCII markdown document at ./specs/<slug>.md aimed at peer engineers and tech leads who need to validate core architecture and trade-offs in 5 minutes. Do NOT auto-trigger on general design questions or coding tasks - this is a deliberate review gate that only fires on explicit request. For LLM-targeted specs (future sibling skill), use /spec-llm instead.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# hybrid_sim
2	
3	Workspace for the Andes hybrid simulator: QEMU 9.x (functional, MHz) + vsim
4	(SystemC + Verilator, cycle-accurate) sharing memory and architectural state
5	across a magic-CSR boundary.
6	
7	Two submodules:
8	
9	- `hybrid_qemu/` - QEMU fork hosting the TCG plugin
10	- `hybrid_vsim/` - vsim sources, including `hybrid/qemu_plugin/` (plugin source)
11	
12	## Quickstart
13	
14	```sh
15	git clone --recursive https://gitea.andestech.com/nick/hybrid_sim
16	cd hybrid_sim
17	./build.sh           # qemu, vsim, plugin (in that order)
18	./build.sh test      # ctest -L hybrid-e2e
19	```
20	
21	Subcommands: `qemu`, `vsim`, `plugin`, `test`, `clean`. Run `./build.sh -h`
22	for the full list.
23	
24	## Host dependencies
25	
26	- C/C++ toolchain (clang preferred), CMake >= 3.20, ninja
27	- Python 3
28	- glib-2.0 dev (for the QEMU plugin)
29	- pkg-config
30	- Verilator (auto-detected; set `VSIM_VERILATOR_EXECUTABLE` if needed)
31	- libraries QEMU itself needs (pixman, slirp, etc. - see `hybrid_qemu/README.rst`)
32	
33	## Outputs
34	
35	After `./build.sh`:
36	
37	- `hybrid_qemu/build/qemu-system-riscv64`
38	- `hybrid_vsim/build/vsim`
39	- `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/qemu_plugin/libhybrid_handoff.so`
40

file.content:
# hybrid_sim

Workspace for the Andes hybrid simulator: QEMU 9.x (functional, MHz) + vsim
(SystemC + Verilator, cycle-accurate) sharing memory and architectural state
across a magic-CSR boundary.

Two submodules:

- `hybrid_qemu/` - QEMU fork hosting the TCG plugin
- `hybrid_vsim/` - vsim sources, including `hybrid/qemu_plugin/` (plugin source)

## Quickstart

```sh
git clone --recursive https://gitea.andestech.com/nick/hybrid_sim
cd hybrid_sim
./build.sh           # qemu, vsim, plugin (in that order)
./build.sh test      # ctest -L hybrid-e2e
```

Subcommands: `qemu`, `vsim`, `plugin`, `test`, `clean`. Run `./build.sh -h`
for the full list.

## Host dependencies

- C/C++ toolchain (clang preferred), CMake >= 3.20, ninja
- Python 3
- glib-2.0 dev (for the QEMU plugin)
- pkg-config
- Verilator (auto-detected; set `VSIM_VERILATOR_EXECUTABLE` if needed)
- libraries QEMU itself needs (pixman, slirp, etc. - see `hybrid_qemu/README.rst`)

## Outputs

After `./build.sh`:

- `hybrid_qemu/build/qemu-system-riscv64`
- `hybrid_vsim/build/vsim`
- `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/qemu_plugin/libhybrid_handoff.so`

117	  ok "VSIM_QEMU=$VSIM_QEMU"

128	unset -f _hybrid_setup_main 2>/dev/null

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Driver: build qemu + vsim + plugin + simpoint, then add build dirs to PATH.
# Source this script to affect your current shell's PATH:
#   source ./setup.sh                       # build all
#   source ./setup.sh clean [target]        # clean qemu|vsim|plugin|simpoint|all (default: all)

if [ -n "${ZSH_VERSION:-}" ]; then
  _SETUP_SRC="${(%):-%x}"
elif [ -n "${BASH_VERSION:-}" ]; then
  _SETUP_SRC="${BASH_SOURCE[0]}"
else
  _SETUP_SRC="$0"
fi

# Wrap the body in a function so strict-mode options stay local and don't
# leak into the caller's interactive shell (which would break zsh prompts
# under nounset).
_hybrid_setup_main() {
  if [ -n "${ZSH_VERSION:-}" ]; then
    emulate -L sh
    setopt err_exit no_unset pipe_fail
  else
    local -
    set -euo pipefail
  fi

  local ROOT QEMU_BIN_DIR VSIM_BIN_DIR PLUGIN_DIR QEMU_SRC PLUGIN_SO SIMPOINT_SRC SIMPOINT_BIN_PATH
  ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "$_SETUP_SRC")" && pwd)"
  QEMU_BIN_DIR="$ROOT/build/qemu"
  VSIM_BIN_DIR="$ROOT/hybrid_vsim/build"
  PLUGIN_DIR="$ROOT/qemu_plugin"
  QEMU_SRC="$ROOT/hybrid_qemu"
  PLUGIN_SO="$PLUGIN_DIR/libhybrid_handoff.so"
  SIMPOINT_SRC="$ROOT/tools/simpoint"
  SIMPOINT_BIN_PATH="$SIMPOINT_SRC/bin/simpoint"

  step() { printf '\033[1;34m==>\033[0m \033[1m%s\033[0m\n' "$*"; }
  ok()   { printf '\033[1;32m[ok]\033[0m %s\n' "$*"; }
  err()  { printf '\033[1;31m[err]\033[0m %s\n' "$*" >&2; }

  clean_qemu()   { step "cleaning qemu build ($QEMU_BIN_DIR)";   rm -rf "$QEMU_BIN_DIR"; ok "qemu cleaned"; }
  clean_vsim()   { step "cleaning vsim build ($VSIM_BIN_DIR)";   rm -rf "$VSIM_BIN_DIR"; ok "vsim cleaned"; }
  clean_plugin() {
    step "cleaning plugin build ($PLUGIN_DIR)"
    if [ -f "$PLUGIN_DIR/Makefile" ]; then
      make -C "$PLUGIN_DIR" clean
    else
      rm -f "$PLUGIN_SO" "$PLUGIN_DIR"/*.o
    fi
    ok "plugin cleaned"
  }
  clean_simpoint() {
    step "cleaning simpoint build ($SIMPOINT_SRC)"
    if [ -f "$SIMPOINT_SRC/Makefile" ]; then
      make -C "$SIMPOINT_SRC" clean
    else
      rm -f "$SIMPOINT_BIN_PATH"
    fi
    ok "simpoint cleaned"
  }

  if [ "${1:-}" = "clean" ]; then
    case "${2:-all}" in
      qemu)     clean_qemu ;;
      vsim)     clean_vsim ;;
      plugin)   clean_plugin ;;
      simpoint) clean_simpoint ;;
      all)      clean_qemu; clean_vsim; clean_plugin; clean_simpoint ;;
      *)        err "usage: source $_SETUP_SRC clean [qemu|vsim|plugin|simpoint|all]"; return 1 ;;
    esac
    return 0
  fi

  step "1/4 building qemu"
  "$ROOT/build_qemu.sh"
  ok "qemu -> $QEMU_BIN_DIR/qemu-system-riscv64"

  step "2/4 building vsim"
  "$ROOT/build_vsim.sh"
  ok "vsim -> $VSIM_BIN_DIR/vsim"

  step "3/4 building hybrid plugin"
  if [ ! -f "$PLUGIN_DIR/Makefile" ]; then
    err "missing $PLUGIN_DIR/Makefile"
    return 1
  fi
  if [ ! -f "$QEMU_SRC/include/qemu/qemu-plugin.h" ]; then
    err "missing $QEMU_SRC/include/qemu/qemu-plugin.h - hybrid_qemu submodule not initialized"
    return 1
  fi
  make -C "$PLUGIN_DIR" QEMU_ROOT="$QEMU_SRC"
  ok "plugin -> $PLUGIN_SO"

  step "4/4 building simpoint clustering binary"
  if [ ! -f "$SIMPOINT_SRC/Makefile" ]; then
    err "missing $SIMPOINT_SRC/Makefile - tools/simpoint submodule not initialized"
    return 1
  fi
  "$ROOT/build_simpoint.sh"
  ok "simpoint -> $SIMPOINT_BIN_PATH"

  step "PATH: prepending build dirs"
  case ":$PATH:" in
    *":$QEMU_BIN_DIR:"*) ;;
    *) export PATH="$QEMU_BIN_DIR:$PATH" ;;
  esac
  case ":$PATH:" in
    *":$VSIM_BIN_DIR:"*) ;;
    *) export PATH="$VSIM_BIN_DIR:$PATH" ;;
  esac
  ok "PATH updated (this shell only)"

  step "env: exporting VSIM_QEMU / VSIM_PLUGIN / SIMPOINT_BIN for e2e scripts"
  export VSIM_QEMU="$QEMU_BIN_DIR/qemu-system-riscv64"
  export VSIM_PLUGIN="$PLUGIN_SO"
  export SIMPOINT_BIN="$SIMPOINT_BIN_PATH"
  ok "VSIM_QEMU=$VSIM_QEMU"
  ok "VSIM_PLUGIN=$VSIM_PLUGIN"
  ok "SIMPOINT_BIN=$SIMPOINT_BIN"

  if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION:-}" ] && [ "${BASH_SOURCE[0]:-}" = "${0:-}" ]; then
    err "script was executed, not sourced - PATH change is local to this subshell"
    err "to update your shell, run: source $_SETUP_SRC"
  fi
}

_hybrid_setup_main "$@"
unset -f _hybrid_setup_main 2>/dev/null
unset _SETUP_SRC

content.content:
1	# Switching-Mode Tutorial and Exercises
2	
3	Status: draft for review
4	Audience: engineer running the hybrid co-simulator for the first time
5	Scope: every switching mode (M1-M8) the repo ships, with copy-paste
6	commands and one exercise per mode.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 0. What "switching mode" means
11	
12	The hybrid simulator runs a program across three phases:
13	
14	```
15	   QEMU1  --(state file + shared mmap)-->  vsim  --(state file + same mmap)-->  QEMU2
16	   (fast)                                 (cycle accurate)                       (fast)
17	```
18	
19	A "switching mode" is the RULE that decides WHEN each leg drains its
20	state and hands off. The codebase ships eight rules (M1-M8). They
21	plug into the same harness; you pick the rule with plugin args and
22	vsim CLI flags. The fixture binary does NOT need to change between
23	rules (one fixture can be driven by several modes).
24	
25	This document teaches one mode at a time, then exercises that
26	combine modes.
27	
28	### Quick map
29	
30	| ID | Name                           | Drain trigger                                   | Used at        |
31	|----|--------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|----------------|
32	| M1 | CSR insn match (default)       | `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` (enter) / `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` (exit) | QEMU1, vsim    |
33	| M2 | PC symbol resolve              | PC equals `_hybrid_enter_pc` / `_hybrid_exit_pc`   | QEMU1, vsim    |
34	| M3 | icount                         | N retired instructions                          | QEMU1, vsim    |
35	| M4 | BBV profile + SimPoint cluster | Plugin emits 1 row per slice; offline clustering | QEMU1 + tool   |
36	| M5 | slice-consume                  | End of slice index K                             | QEMU1          |
37	| M6 | archive emit                   | After drain, write `snap/K/{state.bin, mem, meta.json}` | orchestrator |
38	| M7 | checkpoint replay              | Hydrate `snap/K/` into a fresh mmap, resume      | orchestrator   |
39	| M8 | end-of-program                 | QEMU2 exits via semihosting `SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED`  | QEMU2          |
40	
41	M1/M2/M3 are entry/exit triggers (the "boundary" question).
42	M4/M5/M6/M7 are pipeline stages for the full SimPoint flow.
43	M8 is the natural end-of-run signal (always at the tail).
44	
45	---
46	
47	## 1. One-time prerequisites
48	
49	Build everything once. Each command is idempotent.
50	
51	```sh
52	cd /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim
53	source ./setup.sh         # qemu + vsim + plugin + simpoint
54	```
55	
56	You should now have:
57	
58	| Artifact                                  | Purpose                           |
59	|-------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
60	| `build/qemu/qemu-system-riscv64`          | QEMU binary (phase 1 and phase 3) |
61	| `hybrid_vsim/build/sim_ax45mpv_premium`   | vsim binary (phase 2)             |
62	| `qemu_plugin/libhybrid_handoff.so`        | TCG plugin (state drainer)        |
63	| `tools/simpoint/bin/simpoint`             | SimPoint clustering tool (M4)     |
64	| `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/*.elf`           | Test fixtures (e.g. rt_c_v_regs)  |
65	
66	Common env vars (auto-detected by every demo; override only if you
67	keep these elsewhere):
68	
69	```sh
70	export VSIM_QEMU=/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/build/qemu/qemu-system-riscv64
71	export VSIM_PLUGIN=/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/qemu_plugin/libhybrid_handoff.so
72	export VSIM_BIN=/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/hybrid_vsim/build/sim_ax45mpv_premium
73	```
74	
75	---
76	
77	## 2. M1 + M8 - CSR insn match (the default path)
78	
79	### What it does
80	
81	QEMU1's plugin pattern-matches the encoding `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` at retire
82	time. On hit it drains state to `outfile` and exits 200. vsim does the
83	same on `csrwi 0x7C0, 1`. CSR 0x7C0 is a benign vendor write on Andes
84	ax45mp, so the fixture binary embeds these two `csrwi` instructions
85	at the ROI boundaries.
86	
87	M8 is automatic: after vsim hands back, QEMU2 finishes the workload
88	and exits via `SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED` semihosting.
89	
90	### Inputs
91	
92	- Fixture ELF with `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` and `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` in its source.
93	  Every Tier-2 fixture (rt_c_v_regs, rt_c_matmul, etc.) has these.
94	
95	### Output
96	
97	- Phase-by-phase log line + final `exit 0`.
98	
99	### Run it
100	
101	```sh
102	cd /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim
103	bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_csr_oneshot.sh
104	```
105	
106	Expected last line:
107	
108	```
109	[demo M1] PASS (CSR insn match (M1+M8))
110	```
111	
112	### Drill-down (what the demo does internally)
113	
114	Inside, the demo calls the generic harness:
115	
116	```sh
117	FIXTURE=rt_c_v_regs TRIGGER_MODE=csr EXPECT_RC=0 \
118	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh
119	```
120	
121	The plugin gets `outfile=<state.bin>` and nothing else - that is the
122	M1 default. vsim is launched without `--hybrid-exit-pc` and without
123	`--hybrid-icount`, so its ELF pre-scanner watches for `csrwi 0x7C0, 1`
124	PCs at retire.
125	
126	### Exercise 1
127	
128	Switch fixture from `rt_c_v_regs` to `rt_c_matmul`. The script is
129	fixture-parameterized:
130	
131	```sh
132	FIXTURE=rt_c_matmul TRIGGER_MODE=csr EXPECT_RC=0 \
133	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh
134	```
135	
136	Verify the last line says `PASS: round-trip exit code 0`. Then read
137	`hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_matmul.c` and confirm where the two
138	`csrwi 0x7C0, *` markers live (`enter_vsim()` / `exit_vsim()` helpers).
139	
140	---
141	
142	## 3. M2 - PC symbol resolve
143	
144	### What it does
145	
146	Same round-trip as M1 but the trigger is a PC value (not an opcode
147	match). The orchestrator runs `nm` on the ELF to resolve symbols
148	`_hybrid_enter_pc` and `_hybrid_exit_pc`, then passes:
149	
150	- `enter_pc=0x<addr>` to the QEMU plugin (QEMU1 drains there)
151	- `--hybrid-exit-pc 0x<addr>` to vsim (vsim drains there)
152	
153	CSR-mode matchers are bypassed; the `csrwi 0x7C0, *` instructions in
154	the binary become inert (CSR 0x7C0 is a benign vendor-defined write).
155	
156	This proves you can hand off WITHOUT modifying the source.
157	
158	### Inputs
159	
160	- Same fixture as M1 (`rt_c_v_regs`), but the ELF must define
161	  `_hybrid_enter_pc` and `_hybrid_exit_pc` symbols. The Tier-2
162	  fixtures all do.
163	
164	### Output
165	
166	- `exit 0`, last log line `[demo M2] PASS`.
167	
168	### Run it
169	
170	```sh
171	bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_pc_oneshot.sh
172	```
173	
174	### Drill-down
175	
176	```sh
177	ENTER_PC=$(riscv64-unknown-elf-nm --defined-only \
178	              hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.elf | \
179	              awk '$3=="_hybrid_enter_pc"{print $1}')
180	EXIT_PC=$(riscv64-unknown-elf-nm --defined-only \
181	              hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.elf | \
182	              awk '$3=="_hybrid_exit_pc"{print $1}')
183	
184	# QEMU plugin arg
185	... -plugin libhybrid_handoff.so,outfile=$STATE,enter_pc=0x$ENTER_PC
186	
187	# vsim arg
188	... --hybrid-exit-pc 0x$EXIT_PC
189	```
190	
191	### Exercise 2
192	
193	Compare exit values: run the demo with TRIGGER_MODE=csr and again
194	with TRIGGER_MODE=pc on the same fixture. Both must exit 0 and
195	both must drain the same PC. Use:
196	
197	```sh
198	xxd state_csr.bin | head -4
199	xxd state_pc.bin  | head -4
200	```
201	
202	The PC field is at offset 16 (`hybrid_state_v1::pc`). For most
203	fixtures `pc(csr) == pc(pc)` because both modes drain right after
204	the entry trigger retires.
205	
206	---
207	
208	## 4. M3 - icount round-trip
209	
210	### What it does
211	
212	Both legs drain on retired-instruction count alone. No CSR, no PC
213	symbol. Suitable for CSR-less workloads (an infinite spin loop, a
214	benchmark you cannot edit, etc.).
215	
216	- QEMU plugin: `icount=N1` -> drain at the N1-th retired insn
217	- vsim: `--hybrid-icount N2` -> drain N2 insns after resume
218	
219	### Inputs
220	
221	- Any ELF; `icount_spin.elf` is the canonical CSR-less fixture.
222	
223	### Output
224	
225	- Both QEMU1 and vsim exit 200 with a non-empty state file. There is
226	  no QEMU2 phase because `icount_spin` spins forever (no natural
227	  semihosting exit).
228	
229	### Run it
230	
231	```sh
232	bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_icount_roundtrip.sh
233	```
234	
235	Override targets:
236	
237	```sh
238	QEMU_ICOUNT=20000 VSIM_ICOUNT=8000 \
239	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_icount_roundtrip.sh
240	```
241	
242	### Exercise 3
243	
244	Predict where the drained PC will land for `icount_spin.elf`. The
245	loop body has two RVC instructions at PCs `0x100002` and `0x100004`.
246	Run with VSIM_ICOUNT=5000. The drained PC should be one of those
247	two values. Read the demo's `read_u64` helper to confirm how the PC
248	is extracted from the state file (offset 16, little-endian 8 bytes).
249	
250	---
251	
252	## 5. M4 - BBV profile + SimPoint cluster
253	
254	### What it does
255	
256	QEMU plugin emits one Basic-Block-Vector (BBV) row per slice of N
257	retired insns. The output is a SimPoint `.bb` file. Offline,
258	`tools/simpoint/bin/simpoint` reads it and clusters the rows into K
259	representative phases.
260	
261	This is the SimPoint "profile" pass. It produces:
262	
263	- `simpts` file: 1 line per cluster, `<slice_idx> <cluster_id>`
264	- `weights` file: 1 line per cluster, `<weight> <cluster_id>`
265	
266	Later, M5+M6+M7 replay just those representative slices.
267	
268	### Inputs
269	
270	- Any ELF. CSR-less or not - the plugin emits BBV rows regardless.
271	
272	### Output
273	
274	- `WORKDIR/<elf>.bb`, `WORKDIR/simpts`, `WORKDIR/weights` persist on
275	  disk so that M5/M7 can consume them.
276	
277	### Run it
278	
279	```sh
280	WORKDIR=/tmp/m4-profile \
281	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_bbv_profile.sh
282	```
283	
284	Override the slice and total count:
285	
286	```sh
287	WORKDIR=/tmp/m4-bigger SLICE=2000 ICOUNT=20000 MAXK=4 \
288	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_bbv_profile.sh
289	```
290	
291	Expected rows = `ICOUNT / SLICE`. The demo asserts this.
292	
293	### Drill-down: plugin args
294	
295	```
296	-plugin libhybrid_handoff.so,\
297	        outfile=$STATE,\
298	        bbv_out=$WORKDIR/profile.bb,\
299	        slice=1000,\
300	        icount=10000
301	```
302	
303	`slice` is shared with M5; `bbv_out=` is what activates emit mode.
304	
305	### Exercise 4
306	
307	Run the demo with WORKDIR=/tmp/m4-ex SLICE=500 ICOUNT=10000 MAXK=3.
308	Inspect `simpts` and `weights`. The number of lines in each file
309	must be equal and at most `MAXK`. Sum the first column of `weights`
310	- it should be approximately 1.0 (SimPoint may emit unnormalised on
311	small inputs; the demo prints this sum for sanity-check).
312	
313	---
314	
315	## 6. M5 - slice-consume entry
316	
317	### What it does
318	
319	QEMU plugin drains at the end of slice K (0-based), where each slice
320	is N retired insns. This is the SimPoint "replay" entry: you ran M4
321	once, picked phase K, and now you want vsim to start from the END of
322	slice K (the QEMU drain leaves you with exactly that architectural
323	state).
324	
325	Plugin args:
326	
327	- `slice=N` (insns per slice; SAME N as the profile pass)
328	- `slice_at=K` (drain at the end of the K-th slice, 0-based)
329	- `icount=M` (safety fallback - if K is invalid, drain at M anyway)
330	
331	### Inputs
332	
333	- Same ELF as the profile pass. Same `slice=N`.
334	- K from `simpts` (T4 output) OR from env `SLICE_AT`.
335	
336	### Output
337	
338	- State file drained at the slice boundary. PC inside the ROI.
339	
340	### Run it
341	
342	Using M4's output directly:
343	
344	```sh
345	WORKDIR=/tmp/m4-profile bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_bbv_profile.sh
346	SIMPTS=/tmp/m4-profile/simpts \
347	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_slice_consume_roundtrip.sh
348	```
349	
350	Manual K choice:
351	
352	```sh
353	SLICE=1000 SLICE_AT=3 \
354	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_slice_consume_roundtrip.sh
355	```
356	
357	Inside, the demo combines two modes: M5 for entry (QEMU side) and
358	M3 for exit (vsim side, `--hybrid-icount`). They are independent;
359	each side picks its own mode.
360	
361	### Exercise 5
362	
363	Pipeline M4 -> M5 by hand (no demo script):
364	
365	1. Run M4 with `SLICE=1000 ICOUNT=10000 MAXK=2`. Note `WORKDIR`.
366	2. `cat $WORKDIR/simpts` and pick the first cluster's slice index, K.
367	3. Run vsim manually:
368	   - QEMU plugin `slice=1000,slice_at=K,outfile=...`
369	   - vsim `--resume-from-qemu STATE --hybrid-icount 5000 --handoff-out STATE_V`
370	4. Confirm both legs exit 200.
371	
372	This is exactly what `demo_slice_consume_roundtrip.sh` automates.
373	
374	---
375	
376	## 7. M6 + M7 - archive emit and checkpoint replay
377	
378	### What it does
379	
380	M6 packages a drained state plus its shared mmap into a portable
381	archive directory:
382	
383	```
384	snap/K/
385	  state.bin    # hybrid_state_v1 (8.5 KB)
386	  mem          # full shared mmap (128 MB by default)
387	  meta.json    # schema=1, abi_version=1, mmap_size, slice_index, etc.
388	```
389	
390	M7 replays an archive: hydrate `mem` into a fresh `/dev/shm` file,
391	hand `state.bin` to vsim (or QEMU), let it run to natural exit.
392	
393	Together they decouple "where the simulation ran" from "where the
394	simulation is replayed" - you can email an archive to a colleague.
395	
396	### Inputs
397	
398	- For M6: a plan YAML pointing at a fixture and a drain trigger.
399	- For M7: an existing archive directory and a target model
400	  (`--resume-with vsim` or `--resume-with qemu`).
401	
402	### Output
403	
404	- M6: `$ARCHIVE_ROOT/snap/0/{state.bin, mem, meta.json}`
405	- M7: replay exits 0 (or 200 if the replay also drains); PC advances
406	  past the archive's PC.
407	
408	### Run it
409	
410	```sh
411	WORKDIR=/tmp/m6m7 \
412	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_archive_replay.sh
413	```
414	
415	Switch the replay target to QEMU:
416	
417	```sh
418	RESUME_WITH=qemu WORKDIR=/tmp/m6m7-qemu \
419	  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_archive_replay.sh
420	```
421	
422	### Drill-down: the plan YAML
423	
424	```yaml
425	session:
426	  mem_size: 134217728
427	  mmap_path: null
428	  elf: hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.elf
429	
430	steps:
431	  - model: qemu
432	    trigger: { kind: csr }
433	    on_exit: { archive_as: "snap/0" }
434	```
435	
436	The orchestrator parses this, spawns QEMU+plugin with `outfile=`,
437	waits for the drain (exit 200), then writes the archive.
438	
439	### Exercise 6
440	
441	Use M6 to capture two archives from the same workload at different
442	slice points, then M7 to replay both. Skeleton:
443	
444	1. Run T4 (M4) to get simpts.
445	2. For each `K` in simpts:
446	   - QEMU+plugin `slice=N,slice_at=K`, capture state.bin.
447	   - Python: `orchestrator.checkpoint.write_archive(...)` -> snap/K/
448	3. For each `snap/K/`:
449	   - `python3 -m orchestrator.main replay-checkpoint <snap/K>
450	       --resume-with vsim`
451	
452	This is exactly what T7 (`demo_simpoint_loop.sh`) automates.
453	
454	---
455	
456	## 8. Putting it all together: T7 (the centerpiece)
457	
458	`demo_simpoint_loop.sh` chains M4 -> M5 -> M6 -> M7 on
459	`rt_c_v_regs.elf`:
460	
461	```
462	M4  profile pass    : QEMU+plugin bbv_out=,slice=20,icount=2000
463	                      -> 15 BBV rows
464	---  cluster        : simpoint -maxK 2 -dim 2
465	                      -> 2 representative slice indices
466	M5  per phase       : QEMU+plugin slice=20,slice_at=K
467	                      -> state.bin drained at slice K
468	M6  per phase       : orchestrator.checkpoint.write_archive(...)
469	                      -> snap/K/{state.bin, mem, meta.json}
470	M7  per archive     : orchestrator.main replay-checkpoint --resume-with vsim
471	                      -> vsim resumes, drains naturally on csrwi 0x7C0,1 (M1+M8)
472	```
473	
474	Run it:
475	
476	```sh
477	WORKDIR=/tmp/t7 bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_simpoint_loop.sh
478	```
479	
480	Expected last line:
481	
482	```
483	[demo T7] PASS (Full SimPoint loop (M4 -> M5 -> M6 -> M7)): \
484	   bbv_rows=15 phases=2 replays_ok=2 workdir=/tmp/t7
485	```
486	
487	### Exercise 7 (capstone)
488	
489	Pick a fixture other than `rt_c_v_regs` (e.g. `rt_c_matmul`). Tune
490	`SLICE` and `PROFILE_ICOUNT` so that the BBV pass yields between 10
491	and 30 rows, then re-run T7. Goals:
492	
493	- Find the SLICE that gives a good cluster count (try 20, 50, 200).
494	- Confirm replays exit 0 for every cluster.
495	- Compare wall-clock against the M1+M8 baseline. (T7 should be
496	  similar for small fixtures; the speedup shows on real workloads.)
497	
498	---
499	
500	## 9. Running the whole suite
501	
502	### Sequential runner (with banners)
503	
504	```sh
505	cd /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test
506	make demo
507	# or
508	bash demo.sh
509	# subset:
510	FILTER='csr|pc'  bash demo.sh
511	FILTER=simpoint  bash demo.sh
512	```
513	
514	Output ends with a tally like:
515	
516	```
517	Demo Suite Summary  (elapsed 142s)
518	  T1  demo_csr_oneshot.sh                PASS
519	  T2  demo_pc_oneshot.sh                 PASS
520	  T3  demo_icount_roundtrip.sh           PASS
521	  T4  demo_bbv_profile.sh                PASS
522	  T5  demo_slice_consume_roundtrip.sh    PASS
523	  T6  demo_archive_replay.sh             PASS
524	  T7  demo_simpoint_loop.sh              PASS
525	  ----
526	  PASS=7  FAIL=0  SKIP=0
527	```
528	
529	### CTest
530	
531	```sh
532	ctest --test-dir hybrid_vsim/build -L switching-mode --output-on-failure
533	```
534	
535	Each demo is one CTest entry. Use `-R demo_csr` to run a single one.
536	
537	### Parallel runner (run_e2e.sh)
538	
539	```sh
540	JOBS=8 FILTER=demo bash run_e2e.sh
541	```
542	
543	This runs every `demo_*.sh` (and every other e2e fixture matching the
544	default suite) in parallel under a FIFO job pool. The summary line
545	gets a `demo Np/Mf/Ks` column.
546	
547	> CAUTION: on this 128-core host the default `JOBS` value can saturate
548	> the box and time out. Use `JOBS=8` as a practical cap.
549	
550	---
551	
552	## 10. Switching-mode decision flow
553	
554	When you have a new workload, pick a mode like this:
555	
556	```
557	Is your binary YOURS to edit?
558	  yes -> Add csrwi 0x7C0, 0 and csrwi 0x7C0, 1 around the ROI -> M1
559	  no  -> Continue.
560	
561	Does the binary already export _hybrid_enter_pc / _hybrid_exit_pc?
562	  yes -> Use M2.
563	  no  -> Continue.
564	
565	Do you know an instruction count where the ROI is hot?
566	  yes -> Use M3.
567	  no  -> Continue.
568	
569	You want SimPoint phase-aware replay -> M4 + M5 + M6 + M7 (T7).
570	```
571	
572	---
573	
574	## 11. Common failure signatures
575	
576	| Symptom                                                 | Likely cause                                                        |
577	|---------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|
578	| `SKIP: ... not built`                                   | `source ./setup.sh` not run, or partial build                       |
579	| `QEMU 1 expected exit 200, got 0`                       | Trigger never fired (wrong mode, wrong PC/slice/icount target)      |
580	| `QEMU 1 expected exit 200, got 124`                     | Plugin hung -> `timeout` killed it; check icount/slice_at value     |
581	| `missing _hybrid_enter_pc/_hybrid_exit_pc symbols`      | Wrong fixture for M2; rebuild with the symbols, or use M1 instead   |
582	| `vsim drained pc=0x... not in icount_spin loop`         | VSIM_ICOUNT too small / wrong fixture                               |
583	| Whole suite times out under run_e2e.sh                  | `JOBS` too high; set `JOBS=8`                                       |
584	
585	---
586	
587	## 12. Review checklist (for the reviewer)
588	
589	Use this when sanity-checking the tutorial against the code:
590	
591	- [ ] Plugin arg names match `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c`
592	      (`outfile`, `enter_pc`, `icount`, `bbv_out`, `slice`, `slice_at`).
593	- [ ] vsim flag names match `hybrid_vsim/src/main.cpp`
594	      (`--resume-from-qemu`, `--handoff-out`, `--hybrid-exit-pc`,
595	      `--hybrid-icount`, `--shared-mem-{path,size,base}`,
596	      `--qemu-binary`).
597	- [ ] Demo IDs map T1..T7 to M1..M8 as in `TEST_ROADMAP.md`.
598	- [ ] `demo.sh` order matches `DEMOS` array (T1..T7).
599	- [ ] CTest label `switching-mode` registered in
600	      `hybrid_vsim/cmake/HybridConfig.cmake`.
601	- [ ] `run_e2e.sh` carries a `DEMO_TESTS` array with the seven entries.
602

file.content:
# Switching-Mode Tutorial and Exercises

Status: draft for review
Audience: engineer running the hybrid co-simulator for the first time
Scope: every switching mode (M1-M8) the repo ships, with copy-paste
commands and one exercise per mode.

---

## 0. What "switching mode" means

The hybrid simulator runs a program across three phases:

```
   QEMU1  --(state file + shared mmap)-->  vsim  --(state file + same mmap)-->  QEMU2
   (fast)                                 (cycle accurate)                       (fast)
```

A "switching mode" is the RULE that decides WHEN each leg drains its
state and hands off. The codebase ships eight rules (M1-M8). They
plug into the same harness; you pick the rule with plugin args and
vsim CLI flags. The fixture binary does NOT need to change between
rules (one fixture can be driven by several modes).

This document teaches one mode at a time, then exercises that
combine modes.

### Quick map

| ID | Name                           | Drain trigger                                   | Used at        |
|----|--------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|----------------|
| M1 | CSR insn match (default)       | `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` (enter) / `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` (exit) | QEMU1, vsim    |
| M2 | PC symbol resolve              | PC equals `_hybrid_enter_pc` / `_hybrid_exit_pc`   | QEMU1, vsim    |
| M3 | icount                         | N retired instructions                          | QEMU1, vsim    |
| M4 | BBV profile + SimPoint cluster | Plugin emits 1 row per slice; offline clustering | QEMU1 + tool   |
| M5 | slice-consume                  | End of slice index K                             | QEMU1          |
| M6 | archive emit                   | After drain, write `snap/K/{state.bin, mem, meta.json}` | orchestrator |
| M7 | checkpoint replay              | Hydrate `snap/K/` into a fresh mmap, resume      | orchestrator   |
| M8 | end-of-program                 | QEMU2 exits via semihosting `SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED`  | QEMU2          |

M1/M2/M3 are entry/exit triggers (the "boundary" question).
M4/M5/M6/M7 are pipeline stages for the full SimPoint flow.
M8 is the natural end-of-run signal (always at the tail).

---

## 1. One-time prerequisites

Build everything once. Each command is idempotent.

```sh
cd /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim
source ./setup.sh         # qemu + vsim + plugin + simpoint
```

You should now have:

| Artifact                                  | Purpose                           |
|-------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| `build/qemu/qemu-system-riscv64`          | QEMU binary (phase 1 and phase 3) |
| `hybrid_vsim/build/sim_ax45mpv_premium`   | vsim binary (phase 2)             |
| `qemu_plugin/libhybrid_handoff.so`        | TCG plugin (state drainer)        |
| `tools/simpoint/bin/simpoint`             | SimPoint clustering tool (M4)     |
| `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/*.elf`           | Test fixtures (e.g. rt_c_v_regs)  |

Common env vars (auto-detected by every demo; override only if you
keep these elsewhere):

```sh
export VSIM_QEMU=/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/build/qemu/qemu-system-riscv64
export VSIM_PLUGIN=/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/qemu_plugin/libhybrid_handoff.so
export VSIM_BIN=/home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/hybrid_vsim/build/sim_ax45mpv_premium
```

---

## 2. M1 + M8 - CSR insn match (the default path)

### What it does

QEMU1's plugin pattern-matches the encoding `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` at retire
time. On hit it drains state to `outfile` and exits 200. vsim does the
same on `csrwi 0x7C0, 1`. CSR 0x7C0 is a benign vendor write on Andes
ax45mp, so the fixture binary embeds these two `csrwi` instructions
at the ROI boundaries.

M8 is automatic: after vsim hands back, QEMU2 finishes the workload
and exits via `SYS_EXIT_EXTENDED` semihosting.

### Inputs

- Fixture ELF with `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` and `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` in its source.
  Every Tier-2 fixture (rt_c_v_regs, rt_c_matmul, etc.) has these.

### Output

- Phase-by-phase log line + final `exit 0`.

### Run it

```sh
cd /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim
bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_csr_oneshot.sh
```

Expected last line:

```
[demo M1] PASS (CSR insn match (M1+M8))
```

### Drill-down (what the demo does internally)

Inside, the demo calls the generic harness:

```sh
FIXTURE=rt_c_v_regs TRIGGER_MODE=csr EXPECT_RC=0 \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh
```

The plugin gets `outfile=<state.bin>` and nothing else - that is the
M1 default. vsim is launched without `--hybrid-exit-pc` and without
`--hybrid-icount`, so its ELF pre-scanner watches for `csrwi 0x7C0, 1`
PCs at retire.

### Exercise 1

Switch fixture from `rt_c_v_regs` to `rt_c_matmul`. The script is
fixture-parameterized:

```sh
FIXTURE=rt_c_matmul TRIGGER_MODE=csr EXPECT_RC=0 \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh
```

Verify the last line says `PASS: round-trip exit code 0`. Then read
`hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_matmul.c` and confirm where the two
`csrwi 0x7C0, *` markers live (`enter_vsim()` / `exit_vsim()` helpers).

---

## 3. M2 - PC symbol resolve

### What it does

Same round-trip as M1 but the trigger is a PC value (not an opcode
match). The orchestrator runs `nm` on the ELF to resolve symbols
`_hybrid_enter_pc` and `_hybrid_exit_pc`, then passes:

- `enter_pc=0x<addr>` to the QEMU plugin (QEMU1 drains there)
- `--hybrid-exit-pc 0x<addr>` to vsim (vsim drains there)

CSR-mode matchers are bypassed; the `csrwi 0x7C0, *` instructions in
the binary become inert (CSR 0x7C0 is a benign vendor-defined write).

This proves you can hand off WITHOUT modifying the source.

### Inputs

- Same fixture as M1 (`rt_c_v_regs`), but the ELF must define
  `_hybrid_enter_pc` and `_hybrid_exit_pc` symbols. The Tier-2
  fixtures all do.

### Output

- `exit 0`, last log line `[demo M2] PASS`.

### Run it

```sh
bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_pc_oneshot.sh
```

### Drill-down

```sh
ENTER_PC=$(riscv64-unknown-elf-nm --defined-only \
              hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.elf | \
              awk '$3=="_hybrid_enter_pc"{print $1}')
EXIT_PC=$(riscv64-unknown-elf-nm --defined-only \
              hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.elf | \
              awk '$3=="_hybrid_exit_pc"{print $1}')

# QEMU plugin arg
... -plugin libhybrid_handoff.so,outfile=$STATE,enter_pc=0x$ENTER_PC

# vsim arg
... --hybrid-exit-pc 0x$EXIT_PC
```

### Exercise 2

Compare exit values: run the demo with TRIGGER_MODE=csr and again
with TRIGGER_MODE=pc on the same fixture. Both must exit 0 and
both must drain the same PC. Use:

```sh
xxd state_csr.bin | head -4
xxd state_pc.bin  | head -4
```

The PC field is at offset 16 (`hybrid_state_v1::pc`). For most
fixtures `pc(csr) == pc(pc)` because both modes drain right after
the entry trigger retires.

---

## 4. M3 - icount round-trip

### What it does

Both legs drain on retired-instruction count alone. No CSR, no PC
symbol. Suitable for CSR-less workloads (an infinite spin loop, a
benchmark you cannot edit, etc.).

- QEMU plugin: `icount=N1` -> drain at the N1-th retired insn
- vsim: `--hybrid-icount N2` -> drain N2 insns after resume

### Inputs

- Any ELF; `icount_spin.elf` is the canonical CSR-less fixture.

### Output

- Both QEMU1 and vsim exit 200 with a non-empty state file. There is
  no QEMU2 phase because `icount_spin` spins forever (no natural
  semihosting exit).

### Run it

```sh
bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_icount_roundtrip.sh
```

Override targets:

```sh
QEMU_ICOUNT=20000 VSIM_ICOUNT=8000 \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_icount_roundtrip.sh
```

### Exercise 3

Predict where the drained PC will land for `icount_spin.elf`. The
loop body has two RVC instructions at PCs `0x100002` and `0x100004`.
Run with VSIM_ICOUNT=5000. The drained PC should be one of those
two values. Read the demo's `read_u64` helper to confirm how the PC
is extracted from the state file (offset 16, little-endian 8 bytes).

---

## 5. M4 - BBV profile + SimPoint cluster

### What it does

QEMU plugin emits one Basic-Block-Vector (BBV) row per slice of N
retired insns. The output is a SimPoint `.bb` file. Offline,
`tools/simpoint/bin/simpoint` reads it and clusters the rows into K
representative phases.

This is the SimPoint "profile" pass. It produces:

- `simpts` file: 1 line per cluster, `<slice_idx> <cluster_id>`
- `weights` file: 1 line per cluster, `<weight> <cluster_id>`

Later, M5+M6+M7 replay just those representative slices.

### Inputs

- Any ELF. CSR-less or not - the plugin emits BBV rows regardless.

### Output

- `WORKDIR/<elf>.bb`, `WORKDIR/simpts`, `WORKDIR/weights` persist on
  disk so that M5/M7 can consume them.

### Run it

```sh
WORKDIR=/tmp/m4-profile \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_bbv_profile.sh
```

Override the slice and total count:

```sh
WORKDIR=/tmp/m4-bigger SLICE=2000 ICOUNT=20000 MAXK=4 \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_bbv_profile.sh
```

Expected rows = `ICOUNT / SLICE`. The demo asserts this.

### Drill-down: plugin args

```
-plugin libhybrid_handoff.so,\
        outfile=$STATE,\
        bbv_out=$WORKDIR/profile.bb,\
        slice=1000,\
        icount=10000
```

`slice` is shared with M5; `bbv_out=` is what activates emit mode.

### Exercise 4

Run the demo with WORKDIR=/tmp/m4-ex SLICE=500 ICOUNT=10000 MAXK=3.
Inspect `simpts` and `weights`. The number of lines in each file
must be equal and at most `MAXK`. Sum the first column of `weights`
- it should be approximately 1.0 (SimPoint may emit unnormalised on
small inputs; the demo prints this sum for sanity-check).

---

## 6. M5 - slice-consume entry

### What it does

QEMU plugin drains at the end of slice K (0-based), where each slice
is N retired insns. This is the SimPoint "replay" entry: you ran M4
once, picked phase K, and now you want vsim to start from the END of
slice K (the QEMU drain leaves you with exactly that architectural
state).

Plugin args:

- `slice=N` (insns per slice; SAME N as the profile pass)
- `slice_at=K` (drain at the end of the K-th slice, 0-based)
- `icount=M` (safety fallback - if K is invalid, drain at M anyway)

### Inputs

- Same ELF as the profile pass. Same `slice=N`.
- K from `simpts` (T4 output) OR from env `SLICE_AT`.

### Output

- State file drained at the slice boundary. PC inside the ROI.

### Run it

Using M4's output directly:

```sh
WORKDIR=/tmp/m4-profile bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_bbv_profile.sh
SIMPTS=/tmp/m4-profile/simpts \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_slice_consume_roundtrip.sh
```

Manual K choice:

```sh
SLICE=1000 SLICE_AT=3 \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_slice_consume_roundtrip.sh
```

Inside, the demo combines two modes: M5 for entry (QEMU side) and
M3 for exit (vsim side, `--hybrid-icount`). They are independent;
each side picks its own mode.

### Exercise 5

Pipeline M4 -> M5 by hand (no demo script):

1. Run M4 with `SLICE=1000 ICOUNT=10000 MAXK=2`. Note `WORKDIR`.
2. `cat $WORKDIR/simpts` and pick the first cluster's slice index, K.
3. Run vsim manually:
   - QEMU plugin `slice=1000,slice_at=K,outfile=...`
   - vsim `--resume-from-qemu STATE --hybrid-icount 5000 --handoff-out STATE_V`
4. Confirm both legs exit 200.

This is exactly what `demo_slice_consume_roundtrip.sh` automates.

---

## 7. M6 + M7 - archive emit and checkpoint replay

### What it does

M6 packages a drained state plus its shared mmap into a portable
archive directory:

```
snap/K/
  state.bin    # hybrid_state_v1 (8.5 KB)
  mem          # full shared mmap (128 MB by default)
  meta.json    # schema=1, abi_version=1, mmap_size, slice_index, etc.
```

M7 replays an archive: hydrate `mem` into a fresh `/dev/shm` file,
hand `state.bin` to vsim (or QEMU), let it run to natural exit.

Together they decouple "where the simulation ran" from "where the
simulation is replayed" - you can email an archive to a colleague.

### Inputs

- For M6: a plan YAML pointing at a fixture and a drain trigger.
- For M7: an existing archive directory and a target model
  (`--resume-with vsim` or `--resume-with qemu`).

### Output

- M6: `$ARCHIVE_ROOT/snap/0/{state.bin, mem, meta.json}`
- M7: replay exits 0 (or 200 if the replay also drains); PC advances
  past the archive's PC.

### Run it

```sh
WORKDIR=/tmp/m6m7 \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_archive_replay.sh
```

Switch the replay target to QEMU:

```sh
RESUME_WITH=qemu WORKDIR=/tmp/m6m7-qemu \
  bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_archive_replay.sh
```

### Drill-down: the plan YAML

```yaml
session:
  mem_size: 134217728
  mmap_path: null
  elf: hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.elf

steps:
  - model: qemu
    trigger: { kind: csr }
    on_exit: { archive_as: "snap/0" }
```

The orchestrator parses this, spawns QEMU+plugin with `outfile=`,
waits for the drain (exit 200), then writes the archive.

### Exercise 6

Use M6 to capture two archives from the same workload at different
slice points, then M7 to replay both. Skeleton:

1. Run T4 (M4) to get simpts.
2. For each `K` in simpts:
   - QEMU+plugin `slice=N,slice_at=K`, capture state.bin.
   - Python: `orchestrator.checkpoint.write_archive(...)` -> snap/K/
3. For each `snap/K/`:
   - `python3 -m orchestrator.main replay-checkpoint <snap/K>
       --resume-with vsim`

This is exactly what T7 (`demo_simpoint_loop.sh`) automates.

---

## 8. Putting it all together: T7 (the centerpiece)

`demo_simpoint_loop.sh` chains M4 -> M5 -> M6 -> M7 on
`rt_c_v_regs.elf`:

```
M4  profile pass    : QEMU+plugin bbv_out=,slice=20,icount=2000
                      -> 15 BBV rows
---  cluster        : simpoint -maxK 2 -dim 2
                      -> 2 representative slice indices
M5  per phase       : QEMU+plugin slice=20,slice_at=K
                      -> state.bin drained at slice K
M6  per phase       : orchestrator.checkpoint.write_archive(...)
                      -> snap/K/{state.bin, mem, meta.json}
M7  per archive     : orchestrator.main replay-checkpoint --resume-with vsim
                      -> vsim resumes, drains naturally on csrwi 0x7C0,1 (M1+M8)
```

Run it:

```sh
WORKDIR=/tmp/t7 bash hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/demo_simpoint_loop.sh
```

Expected last line:

```
[demo T7] PASS (Full SimPoint loop (M4 -> M5 -> M6 -> M7)): \
   bbv_rows=15 phases=2 replays_ok=2 workdir=/tmp/t7
```

### Exercise 7 (capstone)

Pick a fixture other than `rt_c_v_regs` (e.g. `rt_c_matmul`). Tune
`SLICE` and `PROFILE_ICOUNT` so that the BBV pass yields between 10
and 30 rows, then re-run T7. Goals:

- Find the SLICE that gives a good cluster count (try 20, 50, 200).
- Confirm replays exit 0 for every cluster.
- Compare wall-clock against the M1+M8 baseline. (T7 should be
  similar for small fixtures; the speedup shows on real workloads.)

---

## 9. Running the whole suite

### Sequential runner (with banners)

```sh
cd /home/nick/work/hybrid_sim/hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test
make demo
# or
bash demo.sh
# subset:
FILTER='csr|pc'  bash demo.sh
FILTER=simpoint  bash demo.sh
```

Output ends with a tally like:

```
Demo Suite Summary  (elapsed 142s)
  T1  demo_csr_oneshot.sh                PASS
  T2  demo_pc_oneshot.sh                 PASS
  T3  demo_icount_roundtrip.sh           PASS
  T4  demo_bbv_profile.sh                PASS
  T5  demo_slice_consume_roundtrip.sh    PASS
  T6  demo_archive_replay.sh             PASS
  T7  demo_simpoint_loop.sh              PASS
  ----
  PASS=7  FAIL=0  SKIP=0
```

### CTest

```sh
ctest --test-dir hybrid_vsim/build -L switching-mode --output-on-failure
```

Each demo is one CTest entry. Use `-R demo_csr` to run a single one.

### Parallel runner (run_e2e.sh)

```sh
JOBS=8 FILTER=demo bash run_e2e.sh
```

This runs every `demo_*.sh` (and every other e2e fixture matching the
default suite) in parallel under a FIFO job pool. The summary line
gets a `demo Np/Mf/Ks` column.

> CAUTION: on this 128-core host the default `JOBS` value can saturate
> the box and time out. Use `JOBS=8` as a practical cap.

---

## 10. Switching-mode decision flow

When you have a new workload, pick a mode like this:

```
Is your binary YOURS to edit?
  yes -> Add csrwi 0x7C0, 0 and csrwi 0x7C0, 1 around the ROI -> M1
  no  -> Continue.

Does the binary already export _hybrid_enter_pc / _hybrid_exit_pc?
  yes -> Use M2.
  no  -> Continue.

Do you know an instruction count where the ROI is hot?
  yes -> Use M3.
  no  -> Continue.

You want SimPoint phase-aware replay -> M4 + M5 + M6 + M7 (T7).
```

---

## 11. Common failure signatures

| Symptom                                                 | Likely cause                                                        |
|---------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `SKIP: ... not built`                                   | `source ./setup.sh` not run, or partial build                       |
| `QEMU 1 expected exit 200, got 0`                       | Trigger never fired (wrong mode, wrong PC/slice/icount target)      |
| `QEMU 1 expected exit 200, got 124`                     | Plugin hung -> `timeout` killed it; check icount/slice_at value     |
| `missing _hybrid_enter_pc/_hybrid_exit_pc symbols`      | Wrong fixture for M2; rebuild with the symbols, or use M1 instead   |
| `vsim drained pc=0x... not in icount_spin loop`         | VSIM_ICOUNT too small / wrong fixture                               |
| Whole suite times out under run_e2e.sh                  | `JOBS` too high; set `JOBS=8`                                       |

---

## 12. Review checklist (for the reviewer)

Use this when sanity-checking the tutorial against the code:

- [ ] Plugin arg names match `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c`
      (`outfile`, `enter_pc`, `icount`, `bbv_out`, `slice`, `slice_at`).
- [ ] vsim flag names match `hybrid_vsim/src/main.cpp`
      (`--resume-from-qemu`, `--handoff-out`, `--hybrid-exit-pc`,
      `--hybrid-icount`, `--shared-mem-{path,size,base}`,
      `--qemu-binary`).
- [ ] Demo IDs map T1..T7 to M1..M8 as in `TEST_ROADMAP.md`.
- [ ] `demo.sh` order matches `DEMOS` array (T1..T7).
- [ ] CTest label `switching-mode` registered in
      `hybrid_vsim/cmake/HybridConfig.cmake`.
- [ ] `run_e2e.sh` carries a `DEMO_TESTS` array with the seven entries.

lastPrompt: write a document for user of hybrid simulator. Focus on  1. how to build and setup environemt  2. how to config the simulator  3. how to switch models using various modes  provide concrete example to…

content.content:
1 # How the Hybrid Simulator Works 2 3 A friendly tour for engineers new to this codebase. 4 5 ## The Problem in One Paragraph 6 7 A pure RTL simulator (`vsim`, built on Verilator + SystemC) is **cycle-accurate**: it tells you exactly how many cycles every instruction takes inside the CPU pipeline. The price is speed: it runs at ~5-9 KHz. Booting Linux, running libc init, and warming up before reaching the actual code you care about can take hours. Meanwhile, QEMU runs the same program **functionally** at MHz speed, but it has no notion of pipeline cycles. 8 9 The hybrid simulator combines them: run the boring parts in QEMU, switch to vsim for the small region of interest (ROI) you actually want to measure, and (optionally) hand back to QEMU to finish. 10 11 ## High-Level Concept 12 13 Five big ideas make this possible: 14 15 1. **Memory is shared, not copied.** Both simulators `mmap` the same backing file (`/dev/shm/...`) as their RAM. When QEMU writes a byte at `0x80001000`, vsim sees it instantly, and vice versa. RAM size becomes irrelevant. 16 17 2. **All architectural state fits in one packed struct.** GPRs, FPRs, M-mode CSRs (mstatus, mepc, mtvec, ...), PMP (16 cfg + 64 addr), V scalar CSRs (vtype, vl, vstart, vxsat, vxrm, vcsr, vlenb), and the full V regfile (`v0..v31`, up to 2 KB) all live in `struct hybrid_state_v1` (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`). That struct is the wire contract every component agrees on. 18 19 3. **vsim only refreshes what the ROI can mutate.** vsim restores into the hart only what it needs to run (GPRs + PC + V state); on the way out it drains back only what could have changed (GPRs + PC + V state). Everything else (FP, M-mode CSRs, PMP, V regfile bytes when the ROI doesn't touch them) **passes through unchanged** from the QEMU 1 snapshot to the QEMU 2 restore. This skips ~85+ DMI transactions per handoff. 20 21 4. **The trigger is a magic CSR write or a PC match.** `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` enters vsim; `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` exits it. CSR `0x7C0` is in the user-defined range. A third form, `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1`, decides at runtime based on `rs1`'s value (1 = exit). PC-trigger mode skips the magic CSR entirely - the orchestrator names entry/exit PCs on the CLI. 22 23 5. **Microarchitectural state is thrown away.** Caches, TLB, branch predictor, store buffer - none of it transfers. We accept cold caches at handoff and rely on a "warm-up window" before recording. 24 25 ## State Categories and Their Transport 26 27 The wire struct is uniform; transport per category depends on what QEMU's plugin API and the AX45MPV Debug Module are willing to expose. 28 29 | Category | QEMU 1 -> snapshot | snapshot -> vsim hart | vsim hart -> snapshot | snapshot -> QEMU 2 | 30 |---------------------------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|--------------------| 31 | GPRs `x1..x31`, PC | TCG plugin reads | DM Abstract Commands (DPC for PC) | DM Abstract Commands (fresh read) | gdbstub `G` packet | 32 | `mtvec` | TCG plugin | DM Abstract Command | passthrough (not re-read) | gdbstub `P` | 33 | Other M-mode CSRs (`mstatus`, `mepc`, `mscratch`, `medeleg`, `mideleg`, `mip`, `mie`, `mcycle`, `minstret`, `mcause`, `mtval`, `satp`) | TCG plugin | passthrough (not written) | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (per `kCsrByName`) | 34 | FPRs `f0..f31` | TCG plugin | passthrough | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (per `hybrid_fpr_names`) | 35 | PMP (16 cfg + 64 addr) | TCG plugin | passthrough | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (each slot) | 36 | V scalar CSRs - `vtype`, `vl` | TCG plugin | DM Program Buffer running `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` | DM Abstract Command (fresh read) | gdbstub `P` | 37 | V scalar CSRs - `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, `mstatus.VS` | TCG plugin | DM Abstract Command, **with Program-Buffer fallback** if the DM rejects | DM Abstract Command (fresh read) | gdbstub `P` | 38 | V regfile `v0..v31` (up to 2 KB) | TCG plugin | shared-mmap scratch region (`hybrid::kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000`); fixture executes `vle8.v` to load | fixture executes `vse8.v` back to scratch; vsim memcpys those bytes into `state.vreg[]` | gdbstub `P` (per V-reg index) | 39 | `vlenb` | TCG plugin | not restored (read-only, fixed by NDS_VLEN) | passthrough (verified, not re-read) | not sent | 40 | Guest memory | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | 41 42 "Passthrough" means: vsim copies the field from the inbound snapshot into the outbound snapshot unchanged. The hart never sees it; the field rides from QEMU 1 directly to QEMU 2. 43 44 ## Trigger Modes 45 46 Two ways to delimit the ROI; pick at runtime via `TRIGGER_MODE=csr|pc`. 47 48 | Mode | Entry into vsim | Exit out of vsim | Source of truth | 49 |------|------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------| 50 | CSR | `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` (or `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` with `rs1==0`) | `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` (or `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` with `rs1==1`) | the binary itself | 51 | PC | plugin arg `enter_pc=0x...` (no insn match) | vsim CLI `--hybrid-exit-pc 0x...` (synthetic hit added via `CsrTrigger::add_pc_hit`) | the orchestrator/CLI; binary is unchanged | 52 53 Three handoff kinds (`hybrid_handoff_kind` in `include/hybrid/insn_match.h`): 54 55 - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_ENTER` - QEMU drains and exits; vsim ignores at retire. 56 - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_EXIT` - vsim drains and exits; QEMU does not encounter this PC. 57 - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_RUNTIME` - vsim decodes `rs1` from the matched insn, reads its current GPR value, fires only when it equals 1. Lets a single binary parameterize entry vs exit at runtime. 58 59 ## Design Rationale 60 61 **One packed struct is the wire format.** Three components in three languages (QEMU plugin in C, vsim in C++, gdbstub-translation layer in C++) need to agree byte-for-byte on what state means. A C struct in a shared header (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`) is the simplest contract that compiles into all three. Versioning is via `magic` + `version`, with `reserved` bytes for forward growth (vxsat/vxrm/vcsr were added by consuming `reserved` rather than bumping to v2). 62 63 **QEMU 1 -> vsim is file-based, vsim -> QEMU 2 is gdbstub-based.** QEMU mainline is used **as-is** (no patches, no fork). It exposes two relevant extension points: the TCG plugin API can *read* state during execution, and gdbstub `-S` can *write* state before the first fetch. Plugins cannot inject state at startup, and there is no `--load-state-from-file` flag. Each direction picks the only working primitive: the plugin drains to a file out of QEMU 1, and gdbstub `G`/`P` packets seed QEMU 2. The vsim side translates between the file and the RSP packets - QEMU never has to know about `hybrid_state_v1`. 64 65 **Passthrough is the largest performance lever.** A naive design would round-trip every field of `hybrid_state_v1` through DM Abstract Commands - hundreds of DMI transactions. Instead, `HandoffController::set_passthrough_input(snapshot)` keeps QEMU 1's drained state in vsim memory; when the exit trigger fires, `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough(snapshot)` re-reads only x1..x31 + PC + (when V) the six V CSRs via DM, and merges those over the snapshot. Everything the ROI is *not allowed* to touch (FPRs, M-mode CSRs except mtvec, PMP) flows from QEMU 1 straight into the QEMU 2 gdbstub restore. ROI-mutates-FP would silently lose those changes - that's an explicit, documented limitation, not an oversight. 66 67 **The Debug Module handles most ISA registers; `vtype`/`vl` need the Program Buffer.** GPRs, FPRs, and ordinary CSRs all have abstract-command slots in the AX45MPV DM - one DMI transaction each. `vtype` is the exception: the V spec marks it read-only via direct CSR write because writing it sets the `vill` flag and invalidates the vector unit. The only legal restore for `vtype` + `vl` together is the `vsetvl` instruction, which has to *execute on the hart*. The Program Buffer is exactly that slot: stage `vl` and `vtype` in scratch GPRs (x5/x6), then execute `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` in the PB. The GPR loop afterwards overwrites x5/x6, so no leakage. 68 69 **Tier-A V CSRs use Abstract-first with a Program-Buffer fallback.** `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, and `mstatus.VS` are spec-compliant to direct CSR write, but the AX45MPV DM may reject the abstract path on a given build. `ResumeDriver::write_csr_abstract_or_pb` tries Abstract first, checks `abstractcs.cmderr`, and on failure clears the sticky error and falls back to a one-instruction PB sequence (`csrrw x0, csr, x5`). The fallback is silent and per-CSR, so a partially-implemented DM does not block the rest of the restore. 70 71 **The V regfile rides shared mmap, not the Debug Module.** The RISC-V Debug Spec does not allocate abstract-command slots for `v0..v31`; it leaves V to optional Program Buffer or DPI extensions, and the AX45MPV wrapper does not implement either. Pushing 32 x 64-byte regs through the Program Buffer one element at a time would be both slow and intricate. Since both simulators already share an mmap, we reuse it: vsim copies `state.vreg[]` into a fixed scratch region before `dret`, and the fixture's vsim-phase prologue issues `vle8.v` from that region to load the real V regfile. Drain mirrors: the fixture's epilogue issues `vse8.v` to scratch, and the simulator wrapper reads those bytes back into `state.vreg[]` after `StateDrain` returns. The fixture is aware of the contract; the V regfile reuses the mmap that already exists for general memory. 72 73 **Missing-register tolerance is part of the contract.** A given QEMU build may not advertise every PMP slot, and the DM may not implement every CSR. `apply_state` in `qemu_handback.hpp` silently skips any name not in the discovered RegMap (`try_write_named`); `StateDrain::read_64bit_register` returns 0 and clears `cmderr` when the DM rejects. Failing closed would block bring-up on every QEMU/RTL revision; failing soft keeps the happy path moving and surfaces real bugs at the fixture level. 74 75 ## Architecture Flowchart 76 77 ```mermaid 78 flowchart LR 79 subgraph DISK["Shared backing files"] 80 SHM[("/dev/shm RAM
(mmap shared)
+ V scratch
at 0x80FF0000")] 81 STATE_Q["state_qemu.bin
hybrid_state_v1"] 82 STATE_V["state_vsim.bin
hybrid_state_v1"] 83 end 84 85 subgraph QEMU1["QEMU 1 (functional, MHz)"] 86 Q1_TCG["TCG insn loop"] 87 Q1_PLUGIN["hybrid_handoff.so
CSR mode: csrwi 0x7C0,0
PC mode: enter_pc=0x..."] 88 Q1_TCG --> Q1_PLUGIN 89 end 90 91 subgraph VSIM["vsim (cycle-accurate, KHz)"] 92 V_PIPE["RTL pipeline
(Verilator + SystemC)"] 93 V_DM["JTAG DM driver (in-process)
Abstract Cmds + Program Buffer"] 94 V_TRIG["CsrTrigger
CSR mode: csrwi 0x7C0,1 (or runtime form)
PC mode: --hybrid-exit-pc"] 95 V_CTRL["HandoffController
passthrough(snapshot) +
fresh GPR/PC/V-CSR drain"] 96 V_DM -.GPR/FPR/CSR + vsetvl.-> V_PIPE 97 V_PIPE -.retire signals.-> V_TRIG 98 V_TRIG --> V_CTRL 99 V_CTRL --> V_DM 100 end 101 102 subgraph QEMU2["QEMU 2 (functional, MHz)"] 103 Q2_GDB["gdbstub -gdb tcp::PORT -S"] 104 Q2_TCG["TCG insn loop"] 105 Q2_GDB -.G + per-name P packets.-> Q2_TCG 106 end 107 108 Q1_PLUGIN -- "drain ALL state" --> STATE_Q 109 Q1_PLUGIN -- "qemu_plugin_request_exit" --> VSIM 110 STATE_Q -- "--resume-from-qemu (passthrough seed)" --> V_CTRL 111 STATE_Q -- "ResumeDriver: GPR/PC/mtvec/V" --> V_DM 112 V_DM -.stage V regs.-> SHM 113 SHM -.drain V regs.-> V_DM 114 115 V_CTRL -- "merge fresh + passthrough" --> STATE_V 116 V_TRIG -- "exit code 200" --> QEMU2 117 STATE_V -- "RSP G + P packets" --> Q2_GDB 118 119 Q1_TCG <-->|"every load/store"| SHM 120 V_PIPE <-->|"every load/store"| SHM 121 Q2_TCG <-->|"every load/store"| SHM 122 ``` 123 124 ## Walk-Through: `rt_c_v_regs` E2E Test 125 126 The canonical demo: it exercises **memory** transport (via shared mmap), **GPR / scalar CSR** transport (via the state file + DM Abstract Commands), and **V regfile** transport (via the V-scratch trick) all in one round-trip. 127 128 Source: `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.c`. Two memory regions live in the shared mmap: 129 130 - `SEED` at `0x80100000` - 16 int32 elements, the input pattern. 131 - `V_SCRATCH` at `0x80FF0000` - 2 KB region, the V regfile transport channel. 132 133 ### What each phase does 134 135 ``` 136 Phase 1 (QEMU 1) Phase 2 (vsim) Phase 3 (QEMU 2) 137 ----------------- --------------------------------- -------------------- 138 seed[0..15] = pattern vle32.v v8..v15, (seed) vse32.v v8..v15, (out) 139 csrwi 0x7C0, 0 vadd.vi v9, v9, +100 verify out matches 140 vse8.v v0..v31, (V_SCRATCH) seed (with v9 +100) 141 csrwi 0x7C0, 1 SEMIHOST_EXIT 0 142 ``` 143 144 ### State that crosses each handoff 145 146 | Boundary | Memory | Fresh-read in vsim | Passthrough through vsim | Fixture-mediated (mmap scratch) | 147 |--------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------| 148 | QEMU 1 -> vsim | `SEED` already in shared mmap; vsim sees it instantly | (nothing yet) | Snapshot held by HandoffController; mtvec written by ResumeDriver | `state.vreg[]` (zero) staged into V_SCRATCH | 149 | in vsim | `vle32` reads SEED; `vse8` writes V regs to V_SCRATCH | -- | -- | -- | 150 | vsim -> QEMU 2 | `SEED` unchanged | x1..x31, PC, 6 V CSRs (DM) | FP, M-mode CSRs (except mtvec written above), PMP, vlenb (all from QEMU 1) | `read_v_from_scratch` -> `state.vreg[]` -> gdbstub `P` per V-reg | 151 152 ### What each step proves 153 154 | Step | What it tests | 155 |----------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| 156 | Phase 1 fills `SEED` via int32 stores | QEMU 1's RAM backend is the shared file (not a private anonymous mapping). | 157 | `vle32.v v8..v15, (seed)` runs in vsim | **Memory written in QEMU survives into vsim**, AND `mstatus.VS` + `vtype/vl` were correctly re-applied by ResumeDriver (otherwise `vsetvli` would have trapped). | 158 | Phase 3 `vse32.v` + verify pass | **V regfile written in vsim survives into QEMU 2.** The `V_SCRATCH` -> `read_v_from_scratch` -> `state.vreg[]` -> gdbstub `P` chain works. `v9 == seed + 100` confirms the *mutated* value, not zero-init or stale snapshot. | 159 | Final exit code 0 | Full round-trip succeeded: shared memory, fresh GPRs, fresh V CSRs, V regfile, and passthrough fields all delivered intact to QEMU 2. | 160 161 A sibling fixture, `rt_c_v_csrs.c`, exercises the V *scalar* CSR survival in isolation (vtype, vl, vstart, vxsat, vxrm, vcsr) using the same three-phase shape but no V regfile traffic. 162 163 ## Authoring a Fixture: What to Watch Out For 164 165 You are writing **one** ELF whose code body straddles three execution windows: QEMU 1 (pre-ROI), vsim (the ROI itself), QEMU 2 (post-ROI). Each window exposes a different set of host-call peripherals and accepts a different subset of state mutations. The constraints below are the ones that bite first-time fixture authors. Each rule is followed by the reason it exists. 166 167 ### 1. Phase 2 (vsim) must be syscall-free; phase 1 and 3 (QEMU) get standard newlib 168 169 C fixtures link against the Andes toolchain's virtual-hosting libgloss (`-lgloss_vh`), whose `_write`/`_exit`/`_sbrk`/... all bottom out in the RISC-V semihosting magic triple (`slli x0,x0,0x1f; ebreak; srai x0,x0,0x7`). Both QEMU launches enable semihosting (`-semihosting-config enable=on,target=native`), so the triple is intercepted before reaching `mtvec`. vsim does NOT implement semihosting -- inside the ROI the triple routes to `mtvec` -> `m_trap_entry` -> trap loop. 170 171 | Phase | Standard newlib I/O (`printf`, `fopen`, ...) | RISC-V semihosting magic ebreak | What you can do | 172 |---------|----------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------| 173 | QEMU 1 | works (via `-lgloss_vh` -> semihosting) | enabled (`-semihosting-config enable=on,target=native`) | full newlib stdio: `printf`, `puts`, `fwrite`, etc. | 174 | vsim | TRAPS (newlib stdio -> `_write` -> semihosting ebreak -> `mtvec`) | not implemented -> trap loop | **register / memory only**. No `printf`, no syscalls, no `_exit`. | 175 | QEMU 2 | works (same `-lgloss_vh` path) | enabled | full newlib stdio. | 176 177 The runtime (`rt_c_helpers.h`) marks the boundary: 178 179 ```c 180 // ===== QEMU 1 window ===== // printf etc. are fine 181 enter_vsim(); // csrwi 0x7C0, 0 -> hand off to vsim 182 // ===== vsim window ===== // syscall-free: registers + memory only 183 exit_vsim(); // csrwi 0x7C0, 1 -> hand off to QEMU 2 184 // ===== QEMU 2 window ===== // printf etc. are fine again 185 ``` 186 187 The forbidden-ness in phase 2 is transitive: anything that ultimately calls `_write`/`_exit`/`_open`/... (i.e. anything that emits via the libgloss syscall stubs) is out. Pure CPU functions are fine: `vsnprintf` into a buffer, `memcpy`, ``, inline asm including V instructions, `setjmp`/`longjmp`. The pattern for cross-phase output is "compute in phase 2, emit in phase 1 or 3." 188 189 There is no third channel; **plan the phase your I/O goes in.** 190 191 ### 2. Only GPRs / PC / V-state survive vsim mutation 192 193 vsim refreshes only `x1..x31` + PC + (when V is enabled) the six V CSRs out of the hart at the exit trigger. Everything else - **FPRs, M-mode CSRs except `mtvec`, PMP, `vlenb`** - rides along untouched in QEMU 1's snapshot. Concretely: 194 195 - Mutating `f3` inside vsim: QEMU 2 sees QEMU 1's value of `f3`. Silently lost. 196 - Mutating `mscratch`, `mip`, `mie`, `satp`, ... inside vsim: silently lost. 197 - Mutating PMP inside vsim: silently lost. 198 - Mutating `mtvec`: lost (vsim writes its own mtvec back in but doesn't drain it; the snapshot wins). 199 200 If your test depends on a non-GPR mutation propagating to QEMU 2, you have to mutate it from the QEMU 2 side instead - e.g. set the value into a memory cell during vsim, then `csrw` from it during Phase C. 201 202 ### 3. The V regfile only round-trips if the fixture cooperates 203 204 `v0..v31` doesn't ride DM Abstract Commands. Vsim memcpys QEMU 1's `state.vreg[]` into a fixed scratch region (`V_SCRATCH = 0x80FF0000`, 2 KB) on resume; the fixture's vsim-phase prologue must `vle8.v v0..v31, (V_SCRATCH)` to load those bytes into the real regfile. Drain mirrors: the fixture's vsim-phase epilogue must `vse8.v v0..v31, (V_SCRATCH)` before the exit trigger, otherwise `state.vreg[]` reaches QEMU 2 with stale (or zero) bytes. 205 206 `rt_c_v_regs.c` is the worked example. 207 208 ### 4. `mstatus.VS` (and `.FS`) must already be set when QEMU 1 drains 209 210 ResumeDriver writes `mstatus` to the vsim hart **only** if the QEMU 1 snapshot has VS bits non-zero (`state.mstatus & 0x600u`). The shared `runtime/crt0.S` runs before the QEMU 1 drain marker and sets `mstatus.VS=Initial` and `mstatus.FS=Initial`. **If you skip the standard crt0**, set both yourself before `csrwi 0x7C0, 0`. Otherwise vsim resumes with `VS=Off` and the very first `vsetvli` in your ROI raises illegal-insn -> trap loop. 211 212 ### 5. The trigger instruction must be 4-byte `csrwi` 213 214 The plugin filters on a 32-bit insn (`size != 4` is rejected). Don't let the assembler emit a compressed form. The runtime helpers wrap the asm in inline strings; if you write the trigger by hand, force `.option norvc` around it. 215 216 ### 6. Stay inside the shared 128 MB RAM window 217 218 Default mmap window: `0x80000000..0x88000000` (QEMU virt's RAM base + 128 MB). The runtime puts: 219 220 - `.text/.rodata/.data/.bss` at `0x80000000` upward (`runtime/rt_c.ld`). 221 - `_stack` at `0x80700000` (override with `-Wl,--defsym,_stack=...`). 222 - `V_SCRATCH` at `0x80FF0000` (must not be touched by your code). 223 - Heap (`_sbrk`) growing from `_end`. 224 225 Anything between `0x80FF0000` and `0x88000000` is yours; above `0x88000000` is unmapped in QEMU 1 unless you also pass a larger `-m`. Below `0x80000000` is MMIO/ROM in QEMU virt and undefined in vsim. 226 227 ### 7. Microarchitectural state does not transfer 228 229 Caches, TLB, branch predictor, store buffer, lr/sc reservations - all dropped at each handoff. Don't rely on a `lr.d` that crosses the entry trigger; it will not see its `sc.d` partner. `mcycle`/`minstret` are not continuous either - keep that in mind if your test reads them. 230 231 ### Where each rule lives in the runtime 232 233 | Concern | File | 234 |-------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------| 235 | Memory map (`_stack`, `_end`, load address) | `runtime/rt_c.ld` | 236 | `mstatus.{FS,VS}` init, sp/gp, `.bss` clear | `runtime/crt0.S` | 237 | M-mode trap save/restore + fatal trap reporter | `runtime/trap.S` + `runtime/handler.c` | 238 | Newlib syscall stubs (`_write`/`_exit`/`_sbrk`/...) via semihosting | `-lgloss_vh` from the Andes toolchain | 239 | Phase markers (`enter_vsim`, `exit_vsim`) | `runtime/rt_c_helpers.h` | 240 | `_hybrid_{enter,exit}_pc` labels for PC-trigger mode | `runtime/rt_c_helpers.h` (resolved by `run_e2e.sh` via `nm`) | 241 | QEMU launch flags (`-semihosting-config enable=on,target=native`, `-cpu andes-ax45mpv,vlen=512`) | `tests/hybrid/{roundtrip_e2e,rt_shared_mem}.sh` (QEMU 1), `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/qemu_handback.hpp` (QEMU 2) | 242 | C-fixture build line (`-nodefaultlibs -lc -lgloss_vh -lgcc`) | `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/Makefile` | 243 244 For pure-asm fixtures (`handoff_roundtrip.S`, `rt_all_gprs.S`, ...) the runtime is skipped entirely: link with `handoff_roundtrip.ld` and inline `semihost_exit.inc` for the QEMU 2 exit. Rules 1, 2, 4-7 still apply; you just hand-code the same checks instead of relying on `runtime/`. 245 246 ## Known Limitations 247 248 These are properties of the current implementation that fixture authors hit even when they follow every rule above. Each one has a documented workaround or is on a future-work list. 249 250 ### L1. vsim mutations to FPRs, M-mode CSRs (except `mtvec`), and PMP are silently dropped 251 252 **What this means.** If your ROI executes `fadd f3, ...`, `csrw mscratch, ...`, or modifies any `pmpcfg*`/`pmpaddr*` slot inside vsim, the value reaching QEMU 2 is **whatever QEMU 1 had at the entry trigger**, not what vsim left behind. 253 254 **Affected registers:** 255 - All 32 FPRs (`f0..f31`). 256 - 12 of the 13 M-mode CSRs in the wire struct: `mstatus`, `mepc`, `mcause`, `mtval`, `mscratch`, `medeleg`, `mideleg`, `mip`, `mie`, `mcycle`, `minstret`, `satp`. Only `mtvec` is round-tripped. 257 - All PMP entries (16 `pmpcfg`, 64 `pmpaddr`). 258 - `vlenb` (read-only constant; not really a mutation target). 259 260 **Why.** `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough` re-reads only `x1..x31`, PC, and the six V scalar CSRs via DM Abstract Commands; the rest is copied through from QEMU 1's snapshot. This is a deliberate speed optimisation (~85 fewer DMI transactions per handoff) under the assumption that ROI kernels don't touch FP / M-CSRs / PMP. 261 262 **Workaround.** If your test must depend on a non-GPR mutation, route it through memory: `csrr` the value into a GPR, store to a known address, then `csrw` from that address inside QEMU 2's phase. 263 264 **Permanent fix.** Extend `state_drain.hpp::drain_with_passthrough` to fresh-read the relevant fields via Abstract Commands, mirroring the existing V-CSR block. 265 266 ### L2. Microarchitectural state is dropped at every handoff 267 268 Caches, TLB, branch predictor, store buffer, and **lr/sc reservations** are not transferred. A `lr.d` in QEMU 1 paired with `sc.d` in vsim will not see its reservation. Cycle-accurate measurements taken inside the ROI will see cold caches at entry and again at exit; use a warm-up window before recording. 269 270 ### L3. `mcycle` / `minstret` are not continuous across handoffs 271 272 The cycle and instret counters jump at each transition (each simulator runs them on its own time base). For a monotonic stream, sum across phases manually; treat the gap as a documented discontinuity rather than a bug. 273 274 ### L4. MMIO inside the ROI is undefined 275 276 Touching device registers (UART, PLIC, CLINT, peripheral DMA, etc.) inside the vsim window is unsupported - the cycle-accurate interconnect may not even instantiate the device QEMU 1 was talking to. vsim's only deterministic peripheral is shared memory (the `mmap` backing file); there is no console, no clock, no semihosting. Stick to register-and-memory work inside the ROI. 277 278 ### L5. Self-modifying code across the trigger is not supported 279 280 The magic CSR write is a synchronization boundary by convention. Rewriting code bytes that QEMU 1 has already executed but vsim is about to fetch (or vice-versa) is undefined: caches and translation buffers are dropped, but the underlying mmap is shared, so timing is the only thing keeping you out of trouble. Don't do it. 281 282 ### L6. Single hart only (today) 283 284 The wire struct carries `hart_id` and the plumbing is per-hart-aware, but the resume/drain path has only been exercised on hart 0. Multi-hart fixtures are not yet supported end-to-end. 285 286 ## Where to Look Next 287 288 - `include/hybrid/state_abi.h` - the wire contract. 289 - `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` - QEMU drain side. See `resolve_handles` (lazy because the dynamic gdb coprocessors aren't registered at vcpu_init) and the entry-trigger callback. 290 - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/handoff_controller.hpp` - passthrough wiring and trigger dispatch. 291 - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp` - GPR/PC/mtvec + V-CSR Tier-A (Abstract w/ PB fallback) + Tier-B (`vsetvl` via PB) restore order. 292 - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp` - DM fresh-read of GPRs/PC/V-CSRs; passthrough merge. 293 - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/program_buffer.hpp` - PB exec helper, `encode_vsetvl`, `encode_csrrw`. 294 - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp` - V-scratch staging / drain helpers. 295 - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/qemu_handback.hpp` - spawning QEMU 2, RegMap discovery via `qXfer:features:read`, and the `apply_state` template that ships every category to the gdbstub. 296 - `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.c`, `rt_c_v_csrs.c` - the V regfile and V scalar CSR fixtures. 297 - `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/runtime/{rt_c.ld, crt0.S, trap.S, handler.c, rt_c_helpers.h}` - the shared C runtime: linker script, entry, trap reporter, phase-aware drain markers. Newlib syscall stubs come from the toolchain's `-lgloss_vh` (semihosting), not from a project-local libgloss. 298 - `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/Makefile` - per-fixture build rules; documents which `-march`/linker-script combination each tier uses. 299 - `run_e2e.sh` - run all e2e tests locally without going through CMake/CTest. 300 301 ## Mental Model in One Sentence 302 303 > Two simulators agree on a memory map and a register struct, take turns running the same program, and use a magic CSR write (or a PC match) as the baton-pass. vsim only refreshes the parts the ROI is allowed to mutate (GPRs, PC, V state); everything else rides along in the snapshot from QEMU 1 straight to QEMU 2. 304

file.content:
